steps: Decomposition: break it into smaller jobs — addition, subtraction, multiplication, division, display, input.
Pattern recognition: notice that all four operations follow the same shape (take 2 numbers, return 1).
Abstraction: ignore irrelevant details (button colour, app name) for now; focus on the maths.
Algorithms: write the steps for each operation in pseudocode.
worked_example.outcome: Four named pillars applied to a real problem = top-band. Without the framework you'd just describe a calculator.

subject: computing
decode: Phishing = fake EMAILS that trick you. Pharming = fake WEBSITES that look real. Spoofing = fake IDENTITY (e.g. caller-ID).
when: Any cyber-threat multi-choice question. Common trap.
example: Email pretending to be your bank → phishing. Site that looks like your bank but isn't → pharming. Caller saying they're from HMRC → spoofing.
context: Phishing, pharming, spoofing — three cyber threats with similar names that get confused on multi-choice. The cheat: phishing uses fake EMAILS, pharming uses fake WEBSITES, spoofing uses fake IDENTITY (caller-ID, sender-ID).

worked_example.scenario: Question: 'You receive an email pretending to be from your bank. What kind of attack is this?'
steps: Email-based attack tricking you into giving information → matches PHISHING.
Not pharming (no fake website yet, just an email).
Not spoofing (the attacker isn't impersonating a person directly — they're impersonating an organisation via email).
Answer: phishing.
worked_example.outcome: Named threat, ruled out the others, gave reasoning. Three marks earned, classic distinction nailed.

subject: computing
decode:
Most tags need an opening and a closing:

...

. Some are self-closing:
, .
when: Web design tasks. Common KS3 trick: spot a missing closing tag.
example:
Title

Body

— both open AND close.
just goes by itself.
context: Web design tasks want kids to spot or fix HTML errors. The classic trap: a missing closing tag breaks the page. Knowing tags come in pairs (with two exceptions) means you can debug HTML at a glance.

worked_example.scenario:
Find the error in this HTML:

Title

This is a paragraph.
steps:
Look for opening tags:

,

(twice — that's wrong),

.

Look for closing tags:

. Where's the ?

Line 1 has TWO opening

tags and zero closing

tags.

Fix: change the second

to

→

Title

This is a paragraph.

.
worked_example.outcome: Spotted the error, named the rule, gave the fix. Top-band debug answer.

subject: computing
when: Any binary-addition question.
context: Binary addition appears on every CS paper. The rules are simple — 1+1 gives 10 (carry the 1) — but easy to fluff under pressure. Memorising the patterns once means you never make a digit mistake again.

worked_example.scenario: Add the binary numbers 1011 + 0110.
steps: Right-most column: 1 + 0 = 1. Write 1.
Next: 1 + 1 = 10. Write 0, carry 1.
Next: 0 + 1 + carry-1 = 10. Write 0, carry 1.
Next: 1 + 0 + carry-1 = 10. Write 0, carry 1.
Final carry: 1. Result: 10001.
worked_example.outcome: 1011 + 0110 = 10001 (which is 17 in decimal — a quick sanity check: 11 + 6 = 17 ✓).

subject: french
tagline: Verbs that take ÊTRE in passé composé
decode: Monter, Rester, Sortir, Venir, Aller, Naître, Descendre, Entrer, Retourner, Tomber, Rentrer, Arriver, Mourir, Partir (+ Devenir, Revenir for the longer version)
when: Past tense (passé composé). With these verbs, past participle agrees with subject.
example: Elle est allée (she went, fem) — note the extra -e. Ils sont arrivés (they arrived, masc plural) — extra -s.
context: Most French past-tense verbs use 'avoir' (to have) as the helper. But a small list of 17 verbs use 'être' (to be) — and with être, the past participle has to AGREE with the subject (add -e for she, -s for they, -es for they-feminine). Get this wrong and the whole sentence is fluffed. MRS VANDERTRAMP is the list.

worked_example.scenario: Translate: 'She went to school.'
steps: 'Aller' (to go) is in MRS VANDERTRAMP — uses être (not avoir).
Subject: she = feminine, singular. Past participle 'allé' needs an extra -e for feminine: 'allée'.
Construct: 'Elle est allée à l'école.'
worked_example.outcome: Correct être verb, correct agreement, perfect sentence. If you'd used 'avoir' or skipped the -e, you'd lose marks. The mnemonic stopped both errors.

prompt: Which French verbs use ÊTRE (not avoir) in the perfect tense?

subject: french
decode: All MRS VANDERTRAMP verbs describe MOVING in/out of a house — going up, down, in, out, arriving, leaving, returning.
when: Stuck remembering the list? Visualise a house and the actions you do around it.
example: monter (go up), descendre (go down), entrer (enter), sortir (leave), arriver (arrive), partir (depart). House logic.
context: MRS VANDERTRAMP is 17 verbs to memorise — most kids forget half of them. The House Trick is the unlock: every verb in the list describes MOVING in or out of a house. Visualise the actions and the list comes back in order.

worked_example.scenario: Recall the MRS VANDERTRAMP verbs using the House Trick.
steps: You ARRIVE at the house (arriver).
You ENTER (entrer).
You go UP the stairs (monter).
You STAY in your room (rester).
You come DOWN (descendre).
You GO out (sortir) — and PARTIR (leave) at the end.
Plus the life-events ones: NAÎTRE (born), MOURIR (die), TOMBER (fall), etc.
worked_example.outcome: Visual story = recallable list. Most kids forget 'tomber' and 'naître'; the house image keeps them anchored.

subject: french
decode: Beauty · Age · Number · Goodness · Size
when: Most French adjectives go AFTER the noun, but BANGS go BEFORE.
example: Une belle voiture (beauty — before). Un petit chien (size — before). Une voiture rouge (colour — after).
context: Most French adjectives go AFTER the noun (la voiture rouge), but a small set goes BEFORE. BANGS is the 5-letter list of categories: Beauty, Age, Number, Goodness, Size. Get the order wrong and you sound like a tourist; get it right and your French jumps a level.

worked_example.scenario: Translate: 'a beautiful little red car'.
steps: Beautiful = Beauty (BANGS) → BEFORE the noun.
Little = Size (BANGS) → BEFORE the noun.
Red = colour (NOT in BANGS) → AFTER the noun.
Result: 'une belle petite voiture rouge'.
worked_example.outcome: Three adjectives correctly placed, native-sounding French. The kid who said 'une rouge petite belle voiture' got everything wrong.

subject: french
decode: C, R, F, L — pronounce these at the end of words. Most other final consonants are silent.
when: French pronunciation. Instantly improves your accent above 90% of classmates.
example: Sport — pronounce the t (it's part of 'rt'). Petit — silent t. Sac — pronounce the c.
context: French has a weird rule: most final consonants are SILENT (so 'petit' sounds like 'puh-TEE', not 'puh-TEET'). But four consonants — C, R, F, L — you DO pronounce. Get this right and your French accent jumps above 90% of the class instantly.

worked_example.scenario: Read these out loud correctly: petit, sport, sac, chef, neuf, mal.
steps: petit — t is NOT in CaReFuL → silent. Say 'puh-TEE'.
sport — t silent again, but r IS in CaReFuL → say it. 'sporh'.
sac — c IS in CaReFuL → pronounce it. 'sahk'.
chef and neuf — f IS in CaReFuL → say it. 'shef' and 'nuhf'.
mal — l IS in CaReFuL → pronounce it. 'mahl'.
worked_example.outcome: Six words, six correct accents. Speakers who know CaReFuL sound French; speakers who don't, sound English. Same vocab, very different mark.

prompt: Which 4 final consonants do you actually pronounce in French?

subject: french
decode: Qui (who) · Quoi/Que (what) · Où (where) · Quand (when) · Pourquoi (why) · Comment (how) · Combien (how many)
when: Year 7 essential. Memorise all 7 — they unlock asking ANY question.
example: Où habites-tu? (where do you live?). Comment ça va? (how are you?). Combien ça coûte? (how much does it cost?).
context: You can't have a conversation without question words. The 7 (qui/quoi/où/quand/pourquoi/comment/combien) cover essentially every question you'd ask in French — and they're in the speaking + listening exam every year. Memorise them as a unit.

worked_example.scenario: Year 7 speaking task: ask three personal questions in French.
steps: OÙ habites-tu? (Where do you live?)
QUEL âge as-tu? (How old are you? — uses combien/quel age framework.)
QUAND est ton anniversaire? (When is your birthday?)
Bonus: COMMENT t'appelles-tu? (What's your name?) — uses comment.
worked_example.outcome: Four functional questions in 30 seconds — exactly what a Year-7 oral exam wants.

subject: french
decode: je -e · tu -es · il/elle/on -e · nous -ons · vous -ez · ils/elles -ent
when: Any regular -ER verb in present tense. Memorise these 6 endings — they unlock most conversation.
example: Parler (to speak): je parle, tu parles, il parle, nous parlons, vous parlez, ils parlent.
context: About 80% of French verbs end in -er, and they ALL share the same 6 endings in the present tense. Memorise the pattern once and you can conjugate THOUSANDS of verbs without looking them up. This is the single highest-leverage piece of memory work in KS3 French.

worked_example.scenario: Conjugate the verb 'parler' (to speak) in the present tense.
steps: Take off the -er: par-
Add the 6 endings in order: je parle, tu parles, il/elle/on parle, nous parlons, vous parlez, ils/elles parlent.
Same pattern works for jouer, manger, regarder, écouter, donner, aimer, chanter — hundreds of verbs.
worked_example.outcome: Six endings memorised = thousands of verbs conjugated. The biggest single unlock in early French.

prompt: List the 6 endings of a regular -ER verb in the present tense.

subject: french
decode: je -is · tu -is · il/elle -it · nous -issons · vous -issez · ils -issent
when: Any regular -IR verb. Note the -iss- in plural forms.
example: Finir (to finish): je finis, tu finis, il finit, nous finissons, vous finissez, ils finissent.
context: After mastering -ER verbs, -IR is the next group. The endings are different (note the -iss- in plural forms), which trips up students who try to apply -ER patterns. Memorising the -IR set unlocks another big chunk of vocabulary.

worked_example.scenario: Conjugate 'finir' (to finish) in the present tense.
steps: Stem: fin- (drop the -ir).
je finis · tu finis · il/elle finit (singular endings: -is, -is, -it).
nous finissons · vous finissez · ils finissent (plural — note the -iss-).
Same pattern works for choisir, réussir, grandir.
worked_example.outcome: Six conjugations from one mnemonic. The -iss- in plurals is the trap; once you've memorised it, you don't fall in.

subject: french
decode: je -s · tu -s · il/elle (no ending) · nous -ons · vous -ez · ils -ent
when: Any regular -RE verb. Note: il/elle has NO ending added.
example: Vendre (to sell): je vends, tu vends, il vend, nous vendons, vous vendez, ils vendent.
context: -RE verbs are the third group, less common than -ER but still essential. The trap: il/elle has NO ENDING ADDED (just the bare stem). Most kids automatically add an -e — wrong.

worked_example.scenario: Conjugate 'vendre' (to sell) in the present tense.
steps: Stem: vend- (drop the -re).
je vends · tu vends · il/elle VEND (no extra letter).
nous vendons · vous vendez · ils vendent.
Same pattern: répondre, attendre, perdre.
worked_example.outcome: Six conjugations correct. The 'il vend' trap claims most kids — they write 'il vende' from instinct.

subject: french
decode: Imperfect = background / used to / was -ing. Perfect = single completed event.
when: Any past-tense question. The trick: one is the SETTING, the other is the ACTION.
example: 'I was eating (imperfect — background) when she arrived (perfect — single event).' Il mangeait quand elle est arrivée.
context: Two French past tenses, used differently: imperfect for BACKGROUND or ONGOING actions, perfect for COMPLETED single events. Mixing them up makes your French sound off; using both correctly puts you in the top band immediately.

worked_example.scenario: Translate: 'I was eating when she arrived.'
steps: 'I was eating' = ongoing background action → IMPERFECT → 'je mangeais'.
'She arrived' = single completed event → PERFECT → 'elle est arrivée'.
Combine: 'Je mangeais quand elle est arrivée.'
worked_example.outcome: Both tenses in one sentence, used correctly. This single construction earns top-band marks in any KS3 French essay.

subject: french
decode: À = to / at (location, direction). De = of / from (origin, possession).
when: Any preposition question.
example: Je vais à Paris (I'm going TO Paris). Je viens de Londres (I come FROM London). Le livre de Marie (Marie's book).
context: À means TO/AT (where you're going); de means OF/FROM (where you came from or what something belongs to). Year 7s use them interchangeably; getting them right sounds instantly more French.

worked_example.scenario: Translate: 'I'm going TO Paris and I come FROM London.'
steps: 'Going TO' → use à. 'Je vais à Paris.'
'Come FROM' → use de. 'Je viens de Londres.'
Bonus: possession also uses de — 'le livre de Marie' = 'Marie's book' (literally: the book of Marie).
À + le = au; de + le = du. À + la = à la; de + la = de la (don't combine before feminine).
worked_example.outcome: Both prepositions, plus the contraction rule. This is the kind of detail that flips a B answer into an A.

subject: french
decode: In French you HAVE 12 years. NOT 'I am 12'. J'ai 12 ans (literally: 'I have 12 years').
when: Any age question. Year 7 classic trap.
example: How old are you? Quel âge as-tu? I'm 11 → J'ai onze ans. (NOT 'je suis onze').
context: In English you ARE 12. In French you HAVE 12 years. Year 7s mistranslate this constantly — saying 'je suis 12' (which makes no sense — 'I am 12 things'?). The mnemonic locks 'avoir' (have) for age, every single time.

worked_example.scenario: Translate: 'How old are you?' — 'I am 11.'
steps: Question literally = 'what age have you?' → 'Quel âge as-tu?'
Answer literally = 'I have 11 years' → 'J'ai 11 ans.'
Don't say 'Je suis 11.' That's the English-thinking trap most of your class will fall into.
worked_example.outcome: Question and answer both correct. Two marks safe. The kid next to you said 'je suis 11' and lost both.

prompt: How do you say 'I am 12' in French — and what's the trap?

subject: french
decode: 70 = soixante-dix (60+10). 80 = quatre-vingts (4×20). 90 = quatre-vingt-dix (4×20+10).
when: Any number question above 69. Belgians/Swiss say septante/octante/nonante (easier!) but exam wants the French version.
example: 78 = soixante-dix-huit (60+18). 95 = quatre-vingt-quinze (4×20+15).
context: French counting works fine until 69. Then 70 becomes 'sixty-ten', 80 becomes 'four-twenties', and 90 becomes 'four-twenties-ten'. It's weird and it trips everyone up. Memorise these three odd ones and the rest of counting falls into place.

worked_example.scenario: Say 95 in French.
steps: 70 = soixante-dix (60 + 10).
80 = quatre-vingts (4 × 20).
90 = quatre-vingt-dix (4 × 20 + 10).
95 = 90 + 5 = quatre-vingt-quinze (literally '4 × 20 + 15').
Belgians and Swiss say 'septante, octante, nonante' — easier — but the exam wants the French version.
worked_example.outcome: Quatre-vingt-quinze. The kid who said 'nonante-cinq' technically said 95 (in Belgian French), but lost the mark because the exam expects standard French.

subject: french
decode: Le = masculine. La = feminine. L' = before vowel sound (le → l'). Les = plural (any gender).
when: Always check gender BEFORE writing — wrong article = wrong adjective ending too.
example: Le chien (m), la chatte (f), l'école (vowel start), les chiens (plural). Note: -e ending often feminine but exceptions exist (le problème).
context: Every French noun has a gender — masculine (le) or feminine (la). Get it wrong and you get the adjective ending wrong, which cascades. The articles are the quickest way to lock in gender, plus they tell you when to apostrophise (l').

worked_example.scenario: Add the right article to: ___ chien, ___ école, ___ chats.
steps: chien = masculine → LE chien.
école = starts with a vowel → L' (apostrophe), regardless of gender.
chats = plural → LES chats.
Trap: many -e endings are feminine (la table) but exceptions exist (le problème, le système).
worked_example.outcome: Three correct articles, plus the apostrophe rule. This is foundational — every adjective and pronoun afterwards depends on getting these right.

subject: french
decode: À mon avis (in my opinion) · Il faut que (it's necessary that) · J'aimerais bien (I'd really like) · C'est-à-dire (that is to say) · D'une part... d'autre part (on one hand... on the other)
when: Any writing or speaking task. Memorise 5-6 of these and slot them in.
example: À mon avis, le sport est important parce qu'il faut être en forme. (In my opinion, sport is important because one must be fit.)
context: Year 7 speaking and writing tasks are usually marked on grammar AND on the use of 'sophisticated language'. Drop one of these phrases into an essay and the marker bumps you a band — they signal you've moved past Year 7 vocabulary.

worked_example.scenario: Year 7 essay: write your opinion on whether sport is important. Use a 'wow' phrase.
steps: Plain: 'I think sport is important because you need to be fit.'
Wow upgrade: 'À mon avis, le sport est important parce qu'il faut être en forme.'
'À mon avis' = in my opinion (sophisticated framing).
'il faut' = it is necessary (Year 9-level construction).
Bonus phrases: c'est-à-dire (that is to say), j'aimerais bien (I would really like).
worked_example.outcome: Same idea, two upgraded phrases, one band higher. Memorise 5-6 of these and you've banked easy marks for life.
